does: This paper introduces SelectiveRM, a framework for training reward models in RLHF that robustly handles noisy preference labels. The core contribution is grounding reward modeling in optimal transport theory, where the authors develop a Joint Consistency Discrepancy metric to align model predictions with preference distributions and introduce a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport that allows the model to automatically exclude contradictory samples rather than overfitting to noisy preferences. The method maintains theoretical guarantees by optimizing a tighter upper bound on clean risk, and experimental validation demonstrates significant improvements over existing denoising baselines across multiple benchmarks.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that real-world RLHF datasets contain significant label noise, extending prior efforts to denoise preference data. It responds to limitations in existing approaches that assume homogeneous noise distributions inadequate for capturing the complexity of human linguistic preferences. The paper connects optimal transport theory—increasingly applied to machine learning for distribution matching problems—to the specific challenge of reward modeling, drawing on recent work combining optimal transport with robustness and denoising. It extends consistency-based learning frameworks common in semi-supervised learning toward the preference modeling setting, where clean versus noisy distinction depends on semantic consistency rather than simple label corruption.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more reliable RLHF by providing a principled mechanism to handle realistic preference noise without requiring explicit noise models. The theoretical tightness of the risk bound suggests potential for analyzing when reward models successfully learn despite label corruption, enabling better understanding of RLHF convergence and sample efficiency. The mass relaxation mechanism could inspire partial transport applications in other robustness contexts where outlier samples are unreliable. Future work could extend this to heterogeneous noise settings, investigate the framework's interaction with policy optimization in the full RLHF loop, and apply similar optimal transport-based selective training to other downstream tasks using noisy human feedback.
